planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring into well-drained soil.

directions: A Fruit Punch selection with rich magenta-purple flowers carried over a tidy mound of blue-green foliage. Increase by division to keep the color true. Grow in full sun and fertile, free-draining soil, and shear lightly after the first flush to bring on more spicy-scented bloom.

planting.note: Set out divisions or container plants in spring into well-drained beds.

directions: This Fruit Punch pink opens warm coral flowers above compact blue-green mounds. Propagate by division so the clone stays uniform. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, deadheading through the season to keep the fragrant flowers coming.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Marketed as Vivid Bright Light, this compact pink is smothered in vivid magenta flowers over silvery foliage and reblooms strongly. Grow from cuttings to hold the variety true. Give full sun and sharp drainage, shearing spent flowers to trigger fresh flushes into autumn.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring into free-draining, sunny beds.

directions: A Scent First selection with fully double, clear pink flowers and a strong clove fragrance. Propagate from cuttings, as named pinks will not reproduce from seed. Grow in full sun and gritty alkaline soil, and deadhead regularly for repeat flushes of scented bloom.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: This Scent First pink bears white flowers with a bold maroon eye and a rich clove-and-coconut scent. Increase from cuttings to keep the markings and habit true. Grow in full sun and sharp drainage, and remove faded blooms to keep the compact mound flowering through summer.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring into gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: A prize-winning laced pink with double rose-pink flowers edged and eyed in deep ruby-red. Propagate from cuttings to preserve the fine lacing. Grow in full sun and free-draining alkaline soil, and deadhead to lengthen the fragrant early-summer display.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into sharply drained soil in sun.

directions: This modern garden pink carries soft shell-pink, fully double flowers with a strong clove fragrance and repeat bloom. Increase from cuttings to keep the variety true. Grow in full sun and lean, free-draining soil, deadheading through summer to encourage more flowers.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring into free-draining beds.

directions: A well-known repeat-flowering pink with fully double, rounded flowers in warm lavender-rose and a rich scent. Propagate from cuttings to hold the form true. Grow in full sun and sharp drainage, shearing lightly after the first flush to bring on later flowers.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into gritty, well-drained soil in sun.

directions: Part of the Devon series, this pink produces clean white, fully double flowers with a strong clove scent on long stems good for cutting. Increase from cuttings to keep the variety true. Grow in full sun and free-draining alkaline soil, deadheading to extend the summer display.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring into rich, free-draining soil.

directions: An antique perfumery carnation grown for its intensely fragrant mauve-pink double flowers. Propagate from cuttings, as this old clone is maintained vegetatively. Plant in full sun and fertile, well-drained alkaline soil, stake the tall stems, and pick or deadhead the blooms to keep it flowering.

planting.note: Set out young plants once soils warm in a permanently moist streamside spot.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on cool, damp compost and give it several weeks of cold before warming to spark germination. Grow it in humus-rich ground that never dries, ideally beside a pond or stream. Nodding, sulphur-yellow bells scented of honey open on tall stems in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in a cool greenhouse and grow on in pots as a frost-free conservatory plant.

directions: Sow onto the surface of a peat-free seed mix and keep it barely moist at a steady warm temperature. Pot the seedlings on and grow them cool and airy to avoid soft, rot-prone growth. Frilled pink, rose, or white flowers with a yellow eye appear over lobed, hairy leaves in winter and spring.

planting.note: Divide the running clumps in spring and replant into cool, leafy woodland soil.

directions: Plant the fuzzy, spreading crowns in dappled shade where the soil stays cool and moisture-retentive. It travels gently by underground runners to form a soft carpet without becoming a nuisance. Rounded, felted leaves back clusters of rose-pink to magenta flowers in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Lift and split the mat-forming crowns after flowering or in early spring.

directions: Tuck the low creeping crowns into cool, humus-rich soil at the front of a border or rock garden. This Caucasian species is the parent of the popular Juliana hybrids and appreciates steady moisture without waterlogging. Stemless, wine-red flowers with a golden eye smother the tiny mounds in early spring.

planting.note: Plant into boggy ground beside water once frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow after a spell of cold and prick out seedlings into moisture-retentive pots. Grow it in permanently damp, rich soil where it will often self-seed to build a colony. Tiered whorls of fragrant golden-yellow flowers, sometimes called Glory-of-the-Marsh, rise in early summer.

planting.note: Set out in damp ground; treat as short-lived and sow fresh seed each year to keep it going.

directions: Chill the seed then surface-sow and keep the compost evenly moist and cool. This small candelabra is often short-lived, so save and resow seed to maintain a stand. Slender stems carry whorls of glowing burnt-orange flowers dusted with pale meal in early summer.

planting.note: Plant into rich, moist bog-garden soil after the last frost.

directions: Cold-treat the seed and sow on the surface of moist compost in late winter. Grow in deep, damp, humus-rich soil at a pond edge where it may naturalize. Tall stems bear tiered whorls of reddish-purple flowers with a yellow eye in early summer.

planting.note: Set out in reliably moist ground once frost danger has passed.

directions: Give the seed a cold spell, then surface-sow and keep it cool and damp until sprouted. Unusually for a candelabra it keeps semi-evergreen rosettes and tolerates slightly firmer soil, though it still wants steady moisture. Whorls of crimson-purple flowers with a yellow eye open through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Plant into cool, damp meadow-like soil after frosts end.

directions: Surface-sow chilled seed on moist compost and grow the seedlings on cool. It thrives in damp alpine-meadow conditions with plenty of humus and never bakes dry. Nodding, funnel-shaped rose-purple flowers cluster to one side of the stem in early summer.

planting.note: Plant into cool, gritty but moisture-retentive soil in a raised bed or trough.

directions: Sow chilled seed thinly on the surface and keep it cold and damp until germination. This nivalid primula wants cool summers, sharp drainage and steady moisture in a raised bed or alpine house. Farinose leaves push up mealy stems topped by fragrant white to blue-violet flowers in late spring.

planting.note: Set out into cold, wet but gritty soil that stays snow-cool through summer.

directions: Give the seed a long cold, moist period before it will germinate reliably. This Rocky Mountain native of wet alpine seeps needs cold running moisture and cool summers to persist. Stout stems lift heads of magenta-crimson flowers with a yellow eye above lush, strongly scented foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings into a very gritty, lean scree with cool root run.

directions: This woody-based Sierra native is tricky from seed, so most growers root short cuttings or divisions in gritty mix. It demands sharp drainage, cool roots and no summer waterlogging, ideally in a trough or scree bed. Wedge-toothed evergreen leaves form spreading mats studded with rose-magenta, yellow-eyed flowers in summer.

planting.note: Set tiny plants into gritty alpine soil in a trough or raised scree.

directions: Sow after a hard, moist chill and grow the small seedlings cool with excellent drainage. This diminutive tundra primrose suits a trough or alpine house where it can enjoy cold winters and gritty soil. Narrow rosettes carry short-stemmed rose-pink flowers with a yellow eye in spring.

planting.note: Plant into moist, limy grit in a trough where roots stay cool.

directions: Surface-sow chilled seed and keep the mix cool and evenly damp. This tiny North American bird's-eye type likes moist, alkaline grit and cold winters and resents summer heat. Slender stalks lift small pink to lilac flowers with a yellow eye in late spring.

planting.note: Grow in cool, gritty, limy soil in a trough; often behaves as a short-lived biennial.

directions: Sow fresh chilled seed and grow the seedlings cool, as this rare northern native is short-lived and self-sows in the wild. It needs cool summers, sharp drainage and open, gritty soil to persist in cultivation. Compact farinose rosettes bear tiny purple flowers with a yellow eye, sometimes twice a year.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings into tufa or very gritty mix under alpine-house cover.

directions: Take short summer cuttings of individual rosettes and root them in sharp, gritty compost. A classic show-alpine, it is usually grown under glass in a pan of tufa and grit and kept nearly dry in winter. The sticky evergreen cushions vanish beneath stemless pink to magenta flowers in late winter and early spring.

planting.note: Set into gritty, humus-rich, lime-tolerant soil in a cool trough or raised bed.

directions: Sow after a long cold, moist period and grow the seedlings cool with good drainage. This eastern-Alps species enjoys gritty, humus-rich soil that stays cool and does not dry out in summer. Smooth, leathery rosettes carry large rose-pink flowers with a white eye in spring.

planting.note: Divide the spreading mats after flowering and replant into cool, moist grit.

directions: Increase it most easily by teasing apart the low mats and replanting the rooted pieces. This little alpine wants cool, gritty, permanently moist soil, as in the wild it grows where snowmelt keeps it damp. Small clusters of pinkish-lilac flowers sit almost stemless over the creeping foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant into gritty, acidic, humus-rich soil in a cool raised bed.

directions: Sow chilled seed and grow the seedlings cool in a lime-free, gritty mix. This western-Alps primrose likes acidic, humus-rich soil with sharp drainage and cool, moist summers. Neat rosettes edged with rust-colored glands carry rose-pink, white-eyed flowers in late spring.

planting.note: Set into gritty, lime-free soil in a rock crevice or trough.

directions: Give the seed a cold, moist spell and grow the young plants cool and sharply drained. A parent of the garden auriculas, it prefers acidic, gritty soil and does best tucked into a shaded rock crevice. Sticky, glandular rosettes bear rose to lilac-pink flowers with a white eye in spring.

planting.note: Divide congested cushions and replant into cool, lime-free scree.

directions: Propagate by carefully splitting the tight cushions, as seed is slow and germination erratic. This tiny high-alpine needs cold winters, cool summers and gritty, acidic soil with reliable moisture. Wedge-shaped, toothed leaves form dense mats topped by surprisingly large, deeply notched rose-pink flowers.

planting.note: Plant into cool, moist, limy grit in a raised bed or trough.

directions: Surface-sow chilled seed and grow the seedlings cool with good drainage. This farinose alpine of limy meadows likes moist, gritty soil and cool summers. Mealy rosettes send up stems of lilac-pink flowers whose unusually long, slender floral tubes give the plant its name, in late spring.

planting.note: Root offsets in gritty mix and plant into well-drained soil or a trough.

directions: These garden auriculas, hybrids between P. auricula and P. hirsuta, come true only from division so detach and root offsets rather than relying on seed. Grow them in gritty, free-draining soil in a raised bed or pot and shelter blooms from heavy rain. Rounded trusses of richly colored, often fragrant flowers appear in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Divide dormant resting buds and replant into cool, gritty, humus-rich soil.

directions: This petiolarid primrose retreats to a mealy resting bud in summer and is best increased by division as the rosettes reappear. Grow it in cool, gritty, humus-rich soil with steady moisture and shade from midday sun. Fragrant pale lilac to mauve flowers with a white, toothed eye open very early, sometimes in late winter.

planting.note: Split the resting buds and replant into cool, moist, leafy soil in shade.

directions: Like other petiolarids it forms mealy winter resting buds and is propagated mainly by dividing those crowns. It needs a cool, humid, lightly shaded spot in acidic, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist. Clusters of clear ice-blue flowers with a small pale eye open low over the rosettes in early spring.

planting.note: Grow in cool, gritty, humus-rich soil in a shaded trough or alpine house.

directions: Sow chilled seed and raise the seedlings cool, as this Himalayan is short-lived and often grown from fresh seed each cycle. It wants a cool, humid, lightly shaded position in gritty, humus-rich soil that never bakes. Nodding, bell-shaped white to pale-blue flowers, deliciously scented, cluster on short stems in early summer.

planting.note: Divide the tiny creeping mats as growth resumes and replant into cool, damp grit.

directions: One of the smallest primroses, it is easily increased by lifting and splitting the spreading mats in spring. Give it cool, moist, humus-rich soil where it will not dry out, such as beside a shaded rill or in a damp trough. Bright rose-pink flowers with a yellow eye sit almost stemless over the tiny rounded leaves in early spring.

planting.note: Set into cool, moist, gritty soil in a rock garden or trough.

directions: Surface-sow chilled seed and grow the seedlings cool and evenly moist. This easygoing bird's-eye relative from the Balkans likes gritty, moist soil and cool conditions and often self-sows gently. Mealy-backed rosettes lift small heads of rose-lilac flowers with a yellow eye in spring.

planting.note: Plant into cool, humus-rich, reliably moist soil after frosts pass.

directions: Surface-sow chilled seed and grow the seedlings on in moist, humus-rich compost. A cowslip-type of the Sikkimensis group, it thrives in cool, damp, deep soil and will often self-seed. Slender stems carry nodding, funnel-shaped wine-red to purple flowers dusted with meal in early summer.

planting.note: Grow in cool, moist, gritty, lime-free soil in a trough or raised bed.

directions: Sow after a hard, moist chill and grow the seedlings cool with sharp drainage. This little northern-Pacific alpine wants cold winters, cool summers and gritty, acidic, moist soil. Fleshy, wedge-shaped toothed leaves back short stems of rose-pink flowers with a deeper eye in late spring.

planting.note: Plant into cool, moist, gritty soil in a rock garden or trough.

directions: Surface-sow chilled seed and grow the small seedlings cool and evenly damp. This neat Japanese species likes cool, moist, gritty soil with good drainage and dislikes summer heat. Rosettes with yellow-mealy undersides carry short stems of rose-purple flowers with a yellow eye in spring.

planting.note: Divide clumps after flowering to keep this old sport true and vigorous.

directions: This heirloom oddity is divided rather than seed-raised so the leafy ruff behind each bloom stays consistent. Grow it in cool, humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in dappled shade and split the crowns every few years. Each flower sits within a collar of small green leaves, a curiosity treasured since Elizabethan gardens, in spring.

planting.note: Divide clumps to keep the striped selection true, since seedlings vary.

directions: Increase this striped selection by division so the pattern stays reliable. Plant in cool, fertile, moist soil in part shade and lift and split congested clumps every couple of years. Cream-white flowers boldly streaked with violet-blue and centered with a yellow eye open through spring.

planting.note: Divide clumps every year or two to keep this sterile heirloom vigorous.

directions: This dainty old polyanthus sets little or no seed, so lift and divide the crowns to keep it going. Grow it in cool, humus-rich, moist soil in light shade and refresh the plants by splitting regularly. Airy sprays of tiny soft primrose-yellow, cream-flushed flowers rise well above the neat rosettes in spring.

planting.note: Divide clumps every couple of years to maintain this sterile green-flowered form.

directions: This unusual green-flowered polyanthus is sterile and must be increased by division to stay true. Give it cool, fertile, moist soil in part shade and split the crowns regularly to keep it healthy. Ruffled, frilled flowers of soft green with a yellow flush open over long seasons in spring.

planting.note: Divide after flowering; this fully double form is sterile and cannot be seed-raised.

directions: Being fully double and sterile, this centuries-old primrose can only be propagated by dividing the crowns after flowering. Grow it in rich, cool, moisture-retentive soil in dappled shade and split it every year or two to keep it thriving. Neat rosettes of double lilac-mauve flowers, also called the Old Double Lilac, open in spring.

planting.note: Propagate only by division, as this double form sets no viable seed.

directions: This old double primrose is sterile, so lift and split the crowns after flowering to increase it. Plant in cool, humus-rich, reliably moist soil in light shade and divide regularly to keep the plants vigorous. Fully double violet flowers, flecked and edged with white, open over the rosettes in spring.

planting.note: Fall sowing gives seed the natural cold period it needs to sprout in spring.

directions: Scatter seed on cool, moist woodland or meadow soil in autumn and press in lightly rather than burying it. The small blue-violet flowers with a slender hooked spur appear in spring and feed early native bees. Let clumps self-sow, or lift and divide crowns after flowering to spread the planting.

planting.note: Sow with the onset of autumn rains so seedlings establish through the mild wet season.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in open grassland soil as fall rains begin, and keep the ground moist through winter. Bright golden flowers held on long stalks open across spring and are the larval host for several fritillary butterflies. Allow foliage to die back and the plant to rest dry through the summer.

planting.note: Set out divisions in cool spring weather into shaded, humus-rich ground.

directions: Plant rooted runners in cool shade beneath conifers or shrubs where the soil stays evenly moist and rich in leaf litter. This low evergreen spreads by creeping stems to form a slow ground cover, sending up small yellow flowers in early spring. Top-dress with leaf mold each year and keep it out of hot afternoon sun.

planting.note: Divide and replant into consistently damp ground as growth resumes in spring.

directions: Give this tiny violet the wet, spongy soil of a bog garden, seep, or stream edge in light shade. Small white flowers veined with purple appear near the ground in spring, and the plant spreads by short runners into a low mat. Keep it constantly moist, as it will not tolerate drying out.

planting.note: Fall sowing on damp ground lets seed chill naturally before spring germination.

directions: Sow seed thinly on moist, acidic soil at the edge of a pond, bog, or damp meadow in autumn. The oval, primrose-like leaves set off small white flowers with purple-veined lower petals in spring. It self-seeds and forms runners in reliably wet ground, so give it room to naturalize.

planting.note: Sow on saturated ground in fall so seed overwinters in place.

directions: Press seed into the wet, sandy peat of a bog or seepage edge in autumn and keep it flooded or saturated. Narrow lance-shaped leaves are topped by pure white spring flowers with fine dark veins. It thrives only where the soil never dries, spreading by runners to make a modest colony.

planting.note: Move divisions into cool woodland soil as leaves emerge in early spring.

directions: Plant crowns in cool, rich, moist woodland soil under deciduous shade where spring light reaches the ground. Bright yellow flowers open early, then the round basal leaves enlarge and lie flat as the canopy closes. Mulch with leaf litter and avoid summer drought to keep this early bloomer happy.

planting.note: Sow in fall on moist ground so seed receives a cold spell before sprouting.

directions: Sow seed on damp, alkaline to neutral soil at the margin of a wet meadow, fen, or rain garden in autumn. Deep violet-blue flowers with a white throat rise on stalks above kidney-shaped leaves in spring. Keep the root zone consistently moist and let it self-seed to build a stand.

planting.note: Fall or very early spring sowing suits this cool-season native annual.

directions: Scatter seed on open, sandy or disturbed ground in fall or as soon as soil can be worked in spring, barely covering it. Tiny pale blue-and-cream pansy faces bloom quickly in cool weather before the plant sets seed and fades. Let it self-sow freely, as it behaves as a short-lived cool-season annual.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seed chills over winter and germinates in spring.

directions: Sow seed on moist, sandy coastal or meadow soil in autumn and leave it uncovered to weather over winter. Large rich violet flowers with a conspicuous white eye stand on stalks above deeply cut, lacy leaves in late spring. It appreciates steady moisture and will self-seed where content.

planting.note: Autumn sowing lets seed overwinter before spring emergence.

directions: Sow seed on moist, humus-rich soil in a lightly shaded woodland edge or streambank in fall. Soft blue-violet flowers with a white center and bearded side petals appear over neat heart-shaped leaves in spring. Keep the ground evenly moist and allow the plant to self-seed into a small drift.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seed receives winter chilling before spring sprouting.

directions: Sow seed on well-drained, humus-rich soil in a sunny woodland edge or rock garden in autumn. Deep purple flowers on upright stalks appear above erect, spade-shaped leaves in spring. This tidy East Asian violet self-seeds modestly and asks only for average moisture once established.

planting.note: Fall sowing provides the cold period seed needs to germinate in spring.

directions: Sow seed on moist, humus-rich woodland soil in autumn and leave uncovered to chill over winter. Pale purple spring flowers rise on leafy stems, and forms with dark bronze-purple foliage are especially prized for shade plantings. It self-seeds readily, so let it wander among ferns and hostas.

planting.note: Set out runners in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant rooted runners in moist, shaded soil where this vigorous Australian creeper can spread into a soft ground cover. Purple-and-white flowers held on slender stalks appear over much of the year in frost-free conditions. Keep it evenly moist and protect from hard frost, or grow it in containers and hanging baskets.

planting.note: Sow in fall and expose to winter cold, as this alpine needs stratification.

directions: Sow seed in fall in gritty, humus-rich soil and leave pots outdoors so winter cold breaks dormancy. Small bright yellow flowers, often carried in pairs, open in early summer above kidney-shaped leaves. Grow this dainty alpine in cool shade or a rock crevice where the roots stay moist but drainage is sharp.

planting.note: Sow in autumn so seed receives the cold stratification alpine violets require.

directions: Sow seed in fall into gritty, well-drained alpine mix and overwinter pots outdoors to break dormancy. Large violet, yellow, or bicolored pansy-like flowers with an unusually long spur seem oversized for the tiny plant. Give it a cool, bright rock garden or trough with sharp drainage and steady root moisture.

planting.note: Sow in fall and chill over winter, as this high-alpine needs cold to germinate.

directions: Sow seed in autumn into very gritty scree mix and leave pots outside for winter cold. Lilac-blue flowers with a golden eye appear in summer on a mat that creeps through loose stone. This demanding high-alpine wants full sun, sharp drainage, and cool roots, so it suits a scree bed or trough.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set plants out around the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter, then transplant into well-drained, gritty soil in full sun to light shade. Deep violet-purple flowers with a slender spur bloom generously from spring into early summer over fine-textured foliage. Shear lightly after the first flush and keep roots cool to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Sow in fall to give seed the winter chill it needs to sprout.

directions: Sow seed in autumn in gritty, alkaline, well-drained soil suited to a rock garden or crevice. Rich pink-purple flowers appear in spring over compact rosettes of glossy heart-shaped leaves. This tidy, non-spreading rock violet wants sharp drainage and a cool position, and it self-seeds gently among stones.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seed stratifies over winter before spring growth.

directions: Sow seed in fall on dry, well-drained prairie or foothill soil and let winter cold ready it for spring. Yellow flowers with brown-purple veining rise over narrow, lance-shaped leaves in late spring. This drought-tolerant western violet suits a sunny dry border or native meadow and dislikes wet feet.

planting.note: Sow in autumn so seed chills over winter before spring emergence.

directions: Sow seed in fall on gritty, well-drained soil in an open woodland or dry montane setting. Yellow flowers with purple-tinged backs and dark veins appear in spring above small, often purple-flushed leaves. Give it lean soil and light shade, and go easy on water once the plant is established.

planting.note: Autumn sowing chills the seed for spring germination.

directions: Sow seed in fall on sandy, well-drained heath or grassland soil and leave uncovered over winter. Unscented sky-blue flowers with a pale, greenish-white spur appear over spring and early summer on leafy stems. It tolerates lean, dry ground and light shade, and self-seeds to naturalize in a wild garden.

planting.note: Divide runners in early fall or plant seed to chill over winter.

directions: Plant runners or sow seed in cool, humus-rich soil at a woodland edge or shrub base in light shade. Fragrant white (sometimes violet) flowers open in early spring, and the plant spreads by runners much like the sweet violet. Keep the soil moist through spring and mulch with leaf mold each autumn.

planting.note: Divide runners in early fall or sow seed to overwinter outdoors.

directions: Plant runners in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in dappled shade where the plant can run into a fragrant patch. Sweetly scented blue-violet flowers with a distinct white throat open in late winter and early spring. Divide crowded clumps after flowering and top-dress with compost to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Sow in fall so the cold-demanding seed can chill before spring.

directions: Sow seed in fall in cool, moist, humus-rich soil in deep shade and let winter cold ready it for spring. Pale lilac flowers with a notably long, stout spur appear low over soft hairy leaves in early spring. This small boreal woodlander needs cool, evenly moist conditions and shelter from hot sun.

planting.note: Sow in autumn so seed stratifies over winter before spring growth.

directions: Sow seed in fall on well-drained soil in the dappled shade of an open pine or oak woodland. Yellow flowers veined with brown rise on tall stems above distinctive deeply lobed leaves in spring. Give it summer shade and dry rest, as this western woodlander tolerates drought once dormant.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seed receives prolonged winter cold before sprouting.

directions: Sow seed in fall on gritty, fast-draining soil in an open, sunny site and let it chill through winter. Striking flowers show purple upper petals over paler, dark-veined lower petals in early spring above finely dissected leaves. This dryland violet goes dormant by summer, so withhold water once foliage yellows.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seed stratifies through winter before spring bloom.

directions: Sow seed in fall on lean, well-drained soil in full sun and let winter cold break dormancy. Golden-yellow flowers with brown-marked backs open in spring above deeply divided, ferny gray-green leaves. This early western violet dies back for a dry summer rest, so keep it on the dry side after flowering.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seed chills over winter before spring emergence.

directions: Sow seed in fall in cool, humus-rich woodland soil and leave it uncovered to weather over winter. Violet-purple flowers appear low in spring over flat rosettes of rounded, silvery-veined leaves that hug the ground. Give it dry-to-moist shade beneath deciduous trees and let it self-seed into a small colony.

planting.note: Set out plants around the last frost into rich, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this near-black viola in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in sun to light shade, spacing plants a hand's width apart. The velvety jet-black flowers with a tiny gold eye bloom from spring into summer and repeat if regularly deadheaded. Shear back leggy growth in midsummer and propagate from cuttings, since it does not come true from seed.

planting.note: Plant out near the last frost in cool, moist, fertile ground.

directions: Set this unusual viola in rich, moist, well-drained soil in sun to light shade, keeping the roots cool. Its distinctive bronze-khaki and old-gold flowers appear from late spring and repeat well when deadheaded. Trim after the first flush and propagate by cuttings or division, as it will not reproduce true from seed.

planting.note: Plant out around the last frost into fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant this classic scented viola in rich, moist, well-drained soil in sun or part shade, a hand's width apart. Soft mauve-lilac flowers with a creamy center and light fragrance bloom from spring through summer with regular deadheading. Cut plants back by half after the main flush and renew from cuttings every few years.

planting.note: Set plants out near the last frost in cool, fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this cheerful old viola in rich, moist, well-drained soil in sun to part shade. Its two-toned flowers pair bright yellow lower petals with chestnut-brown upper petals from spring into summer. Deadhead often and shear after the first flush, propagating by cuttings or division to keep the strain going.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring or fall into moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant this common blue violet selection in moist, fertile soil in sun to part shade, spacing the clumps evenly. Its white flowers are speckled and splashed with blue freckles, opening in spring above heart-shaped leaves. It self-seeds and spreads readily, so divide crowded clumps and pull unwanted seedlings to keep it in bounds.

planting.note: Divide and set out rooted runners in early fall or spring.

directions: Plant rooted runners in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in dappled shade where the plant can spread into a fragrant patch. Deep violet-blue, richly scented flowers appear from late winter into spring on this old garden sweet violet. Divide congested clumps after flowering and mulch with leaf mold each autumn to sustain vigor.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring once the soil has warmed and hard frost has passed.

directions: Plant divisions at the depth they grew before, keeping the crown level with the soil surface. Water steadily through summer and stake taller clumps in exposed sites to prevent flopping. Semi-double rose-pink daisies open from late summer into autumn. Cut plants back after bloom and divide every third year to keep the clump vigorous.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into rich, moisture-retentive ground.

directions: Give this cultivar an open, sunny spot with good air movement to reduce mildew pressure. Set the crown at soil level and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. Large clear-pink double flowers cover the plant in early autumn. Deadhead and shear back after flowering, lifting and splitting congested clumps in spring.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions after the last frost in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Position in full sun with even moisture and space plants for airflow. Keep the root zone cool with a light mulch and avoid letting the ground dry out in summer. Semi-double, glowing red-crimson blooms with yellow centers appear from late summer. Shear spent stems and divide the clump every two to three years.

planting.note: Set out compact divisions in spring, ideal for the front of a border.

directions: This shorter cultivar needs no staking but appreciates full sun and steady moisture. Plant with the crown flush to the surface and water through dry spells. Rich ruby-red semi-double flowers smother the mound in early autumn. Trim back after bloom and divide clumps in spring to refresh vigor.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a sunny, fertile border.

directions: Grow in full sun with humus-rich, evenly moist soil for the best flower size. Plant divisions at their previous depth and keep watered during establishment. Large lavender-blue semi-double daisies open in September and October. Cut back hard after flowering and lift crowded clumps every few springs.

planting.note: Plant tall spring divisions where they can be staked or supported.

directions: This vigorous tall selection benefits from staking and generous spacing for airflow. Plant the crown at soil level in rich, moisture-holding ground and water in dry weather. Airy sprays of small white daisies cover the upper stems in autumn. Shear after bloom and divide every second or third year to control the spread.

planting.note: Set out compact spring divisions at the border edge.

directions: A tidy dwarf that needs no support, this cultivar wants sun and steady soil moisture. Plant with the crown level and mulch lightly to retain water. Deep violet-purple flowers with golden eyes cover the plant in early fall. Cut back after bloom and divide the compact clump every couple of springs.

planting.note: Transplant spring divisions into deep, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Give this tall selection full sun and rich, consistently moist ground for strong stems. Pinch the shoots in early summer to reduce height and encourage branching, or stake the clump. Clear salmon-pink daisies open over a long autumn season. Cut back after frost and divide every two to three years to prevent bare centers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun with room to reach full height.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in fertile, evenly moist ground and water well while establishing. Pinch back once or twice before midsummer to build a sturdier, bushier plant. Semi-double, rich rose-pink flowers cover the stems in late summer and fall. Divide congested clumps in spring to keep them healthy and floriferous.

planting.note: Transplant in spring where tall stems can be supported.

directions: This tall, upright cultivar wants full sun and deep, moisture-retentive soil. Stake or pinch the stems early to manage height and improve stability. Masses of deep violet-purple daisies with yellow eyes appear from late summer into fall. Cut back after bloom and divide the clump every few springs.

planting.note: Set out compact spring divisions near the front of a bed.

directions: A naturally domed, compact selection that needs no staking or pinching. Plant in full sun with steady moisture and set the crown flush with the soil. Bright rose-pink daisies smother the mound from late summer through fall. Shear back after bloom and divide every two to three years to maintain the tidy shape.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in full sun and fertile soil.

directions: Grow in full sun with rich, moisture-holding ground and space clumps for airflow. Pinch the stems in early summer for a bushier habit or provide light support. Warm rose to salmon-pink flowers open across a long autumn display. Cut back after frost and lift and divide crowded clumps in spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into sharply drained, alkaline-tolerant soil.

directions: Also sold as Violet Queen, this compact aster thrives in full sun and well-drained, even chalky soil. Avoid waterlogged winter conditions and divide in spring rather than fall. Deep violet-purple daisies with yellow centers bloom from late summer into autumn. Trim spent flowers and lift clumps every few years to renew them.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in a warm, freely draining site.

directions: This robust Italian aster prefers full sun and gritty, well-drained soil that stays dry in winter. Set the crown level with the surface and keep watering modest once established. Large lavender-violet flowers with golden centers appear over a long autumn period. Divide only in spring, and shear lightly after bloom to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Set out spring divisions in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Grow this pink Italian aster in a sunny, sharply drained spot that avoids winter wet. Plant at the original depth and water sparingly once roots take hold. Soft clear-pink daisies with yellow eyes open from late summer to fall. Divide in spring and trim after flowering to keep the clump compact.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into freely draining, sun-baked soil.

directions: This mildew-resistant Italian aster does best in full sun and dry, well-drained ground. Keep the crown at soil level and avoid soggy conditions, especially over winter. Lavender-blue daisies with bright yellow centers bloom well into autumn. Divide clumps in spring and cut back lightly after the flowers fade.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, sharply drained rock-garden soil.

directions: This early-blooming alpine aster suits rock gardens and border edges in full sun with fast drainage. Set plants at soil level and avoid heavy, wet ground that rots the crown. Large pale lavender-blue daisies open in late spring and early summer, well ahead of autumn asters. Shear lightly after bloom and divide congested clumps in spring.

planting.note: Set out spring plants in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: A compact alpine aster that flowers in late spring, best in full sun with sharp drainage. Plant with the crown level and keep moisture modest, particularly in winter. Semi-double, clear pink daisies with golden centers cover the low mound. Trim after bloom and divide every few springs to keep the plant tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, freely draining position.

directions: Grow this deep-flowered alpine aster in full sun with gritty, sharply drained soil. Set the crown flush with the surface and avoid winter wet to prevent rot. Rich deep blue-violet daisies with yellow eyes open in late spring. Deadhead after flowering and lift and divide crowded clumps in spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into moist but well-drained soil.

directions: Also sold as Wartburg Star, this early aster likes full sun to light shade and cool, moist but well-drained soil. Plant at the original depth and avoid drought during establishment. Large single lavender-blue daisies with orange centers appear in early summer. Deadhead to prolong bloom and divide clumps in spring.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in cool, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: This early-summer aster prefers sun to partial shade with consistently moist, well-drained soil. Set the crown level and keep the root zone cool with a light mulch. Violet-blue single daisies centered with orange discs open ahead of the fall asters. Remove faded flowers and divide the clump in spring to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: One of the Swiss-named Frikart hybrids, this aster wants full sun and freely draining, fertile soil. Plant at the original depth and mulch to protect the crown over winter in colder zones. Lavender-blue daisies with yellow centers bloom over a very long summer-to-fall season. Divide in spring only, and deadhead to extend the display.

planting.note: Plant tall spring divisions where they can be staked.

directions: This dramatic tall aster is prized for near-black stems and dark foliage, best in full sun. Give it room and support, or pinch in early summer to reduce its considerable height. Lilac-blue daisies with yellow centers cover the plant in autumn. Cut back after frost and divide large clumps in spring.

planting.note: Set out spring divisions in full sun for cutting rows or borders.

directions: A classic florist's aster grown for its airy sprays of tiny flowers, best in full sun and well-drained soil. Plant at the previous depth and provide light support in cutting rows. Clouds of small white daisies with yellow eyes open in early autumn. Harvest stems when a portion of flowers is open, and divide clumps in spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into average, well-drained soil.

directions: Grown for its dark purple-black foliage, this calico aster does well in full sun to light shade. Plant at soil level and water moderately during dry spells. Tiny white daisies with rosy-red centers stud the dark stems in autumn. Cut back after bloom and divide the clump every two to three years.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in full sun with average soil.

directions: This calico aster has a distinctive tiered, horizontally branching habit and small leaves that flush bronze. Grow in full sun to part shade and water moderately. Countless tiny pale flowers with pink-red centers appear in fall along the layered branches. Shear back after bloom and divide the clump in spring as needed.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: A celebrated cordifolius-type hybrid that produces sheets of small flowers, best in full sun and rich soil. Set the crown level and keep evenly moist for the strongest bloom. Clouds of clear lavender-blue daisies with yellow eyes cover the plant in autumn. Provide light support if needed and divide clumps every few springs.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: This graceful small-flowered aster forms an upright cloud of bloom in full sun. Plant at the previous depth in fertile soil and keep watered during establishment. A haze of tiny pale lilac-blue daisies with yellow centers opens in autumn. Support taller clumps lightly and divide in spring to keep them healthy.

planting.note: Transplant tall spring divisions with room to spread.

directions: A tall, robust pink-flowered aster whose name means morning glow, best in full sun and fertile ground. Give it space and pinch or support the stems to manage height. Masses of small soft-pink daisies with yellow centers cover the upper stems in autumn. Cut back after bloom and divide the vigorous clump every few springs.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in full sun and average soil.

directions: This reliable small-flowered aster carries airy sprays of bloom on well-branched stems in full sun. Set the crown level and water moderately through the growing season. Tiny lilac-pink daisies with centers that age from yellow to rose open in autumn. Deadhead lightly and divide the clump in spring to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Set out compact spring divisions at the front of a bed.

directions: This mildew-resistant dwarf aster forms a neat mound that needs no staking. Plant in full sun with average, well-drained soil and water during dry spells. Purple daisies with yellow centers cover the compact plant in early fall. Shear back after bloom and divide the clump every few springs.

planting.note: Plant compact spring divisions in full sun.

directions: A tidy, disease-resistant dwarf aster ideal for edging and containers in full sun. Set the crown flush with the soil and keep moisture steady. Clear pink daisies with yellow eyes smother the mound in early autumn. Trim after flowering and divide congested clumps in spring.

planting.note: Set out compact spring divisions in a sunny border.

directions: This compact, mildew-resistant aster makes a rounded mound needing no support. Plant in full sun with average soil and provide even moisture through summer. Blue-lavender daisies with yellow centers cover the plant in early fall. Cut back after bloom and divide every couple of springs to keep it dense.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a shaded, humus-rich site.

directions: A refined selection of white wood aster prized for its dark, near-black stems and shade tolerance. Plant in partial to full shade with humus-rich, well-drained soil and set the crown level. Small white daisies with reddish centers open above the dark stems in late summer and fall. Divide clumps in spring and cut back after bloom.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in sun or light shade with good drainage.

directions: Formerly listed under Aster macrophyllus, this durable aster tolerates sun or partial shade and dry soil. Set the crown level in well-drained ground and water while establishing. Flat-topped clusters of violet-blue daisies with yellow centers open in late summer. Cut back after bloom and divide the clump in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; give it room as it spreads by rhizomes.

directions: This tall, late native aster spreads by rhizomes, so site it where it can naturalize or contain it in a bed. Plant in full sun and average to moist soil, watering during establishment. Pale blue to lavender daisies with yellow centers open very late in the season. Cut back after frost and divide clumps in spring to control the spread.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and well-drained, lean soil.

directions: This elegant prairie native carries wiry, well-branched stems above smooth foliage, thriving in full sun and dry soil. Plant at the previous depth in lean, well-drained ground and water sparingly once established. Delicate lavender-blue daisies with yellow centers open in autumn on airy stems. Cut back after frost and divide clumps in spring to renew them.

planting.note: Sow on a prepared bed once the soil warms in spring, or set out divisions.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface of gritty, fast-draining soil and barely cover it, keeping the bed just moist until sprouts appear. Give this plains native full sun and lean ground, watering only during extended drought once established. Enjoy the aromatic silvery foliage all season; the small yellowish flower heads open on tall stems in late summer.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm, sandy ground in spring.

directions: Scatter seed on loose sandy soil and firm it lightly rather than burying it deep. Thread-like silvery leaves give this shrub a soft, feathery look, and it thrives on neglect in hot, dry, sun-baked sites. Tiny nodding flower heads appear on the upper stems from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Set out young plants after the last frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow the dust-fine seed indoors and grow on before transplanting to a warm, well-drained spot. This vigorous grey-green herb is strongly camphor-scented and can be cut back hard each spring to keep it bushy. In frost-prone gardens grow it in a container and shelter it over winter; small cream flower heads appear in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; it spreads readily by rhizomes.

directions: Set rooted divisions at their original depth in ordinary garden soil, ideally where its running roots can be contained. The aromatic leaves are silvery beneath and are the traditional yomogi herb, harvested young in spring and early summer. Cut back after the small greenish flower spikes form in late summer to limit self-seeding.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in the West.

directions: Plant divisions or rooted rhizome pieces where the running roots have room to colonize a moist swale or streambank. Leaves are dark green above and silvery-white beneath, releasing a sage-like scent when brushed. This tough California native tolerates seasonal dryness and produces slender greenish flower stalks by late summer.

planting.note: Set out cutting-grown plants in fall or spring on the mild West Coast.

directions: This selection is grown from cuttings and forms a dense, low mound of finely cut silver foliage. Plant in fast-draining soil with full sun and avoid summer overhead watering, which can rot the crown. Shear lightly after the small yellow flower heads fade in summer to keep the cushion tidy and compact.

planting.note: Direct sow on lean, sandy ground in spring.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on dry, gritty soil and keep barely moist until germination. The finely divided leaves form a low tuft that sends up wiry, reddish flowering stems in summer. It asks for nothing but sun and sharp drainage, making it ideal for sandy banks and gravel gardens.

planting.note: Sow in spring in free-draining, even slightly saline soil.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty soil and press in gently, as light aids germination. This coastal species carries woolly, grey-white aromatic foliage and shrugs off wind and salt spray. Small yellowish flower heads nod along the stems in late summer; trim back in spring to renew the mound.

planting.note: Sow in fall so seed experiences winter chilling, or in early spring.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of rocky, alkaline soil in fall and let winter cold break dormancy. This compact high-desert shrub carries small grey-green aromatic leaves and prefers very lean, dry conditions. Give it full sun and excellent drainage; tiny flower heads appear low in the foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall for a cold period, or refrigerate seed before spring sowing.

directions: Sow on the surface of a very gritty alpine mix and expose seed to winter cold to trigger germination. This tiny cushion-forming wormwood needs sharp drainage, cool roots, and full mountain sun. Its silvery aromatic tufts send up short flowering stems bearing rounded golden heads in summer, prized for génépi liqueur.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring, or plant divisions.

directions: Press seed onto the soil surface in spring, as it needs light to sprout. This upright species forms a bushy mound of finely cut, feathery grey-green foliage that stays neat with a hard spring cutback. Grow in full sun and dry soil; slender flowering stems carry small yellow heads by late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Set divisions with the crown at soil level in full sun and average, well-drained soil. Tall stems carry broad, flat plates of deep golden-yellow flowers that are excellent for cutting and drying. Stake in windy sites and cut spent stems to the base to tidy the clump after bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost and transplant out after it passes.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors and press it in, moving seedlings out once frost danger passes. The toothed, sweetly aromatic leaves have long been used as a culinary herb known as English Mace. Clusters of small bright yellow flowers open through summer; shear after bloom to encourage a second flush.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant divisions in lean, well-drained soil and full sun for the sturdiest stems. Grey-green ferny foliage sets off soft, pale sulfur-yellow flower plates that gently fade to cream. Deadhead through summer to prolong bloom and lift and divide the clump every few years to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Plant divisions or set out young plants in spring.

directions: Set plants in full sun and average, well-drained soil where they will spread into a colony. Flat heads of deep rose-pink flowers age to softer shades and draw butterflies through summer. Cut back after the first flush and divide clumps every couple of years to maintain flower quality.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant this Tutti Frutti selection from division in full sun and free-draining soil. Broad flower plates open in soft lilac-pink and gradually shift to paler pastel tones as they mature. Deadhead spent heads to keep it blooming and cut the whole clump back in late fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Set divisions of this Tutti Frutti yarrow in full sun and lean, well-drained ground. Flat clusters open in warm apricot-pink with a paler yellow eye, softening as the blooms age. Remove faded heads to extend the display and shear the plant back after its main summer flush.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; tolerates more moisture than most yarrows.

directions: Plant divisions in full sun; unlike its drought-loving relatives this species accepts evenly moist soil. Its glossy, toothed green leaves differ from the ferny yarrows and set off flat heads of soft pink flowers. Extremely cold-hardy, it blooms in late summer and can be cut back after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sharply drained rock garden.

directions: Tuck small divisions into gritty, fast-draining soil in a rock garden or trough with full sun. This mat-forming hybrid spreads finely cut silvery foliage across the ground and dislikes winter wet. Loose sprays of small white daisy-like flowers rise just above the mat in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, gritty rock garden.

directions: Grow from division or surface-sown seed in very sharp-draining, gritty soil and full sun. This Greek alpine forms a low rosette of silvery, deeply lobed leaves that hug the ground. Short stems carry small clusters of white flowers in early summer; protect the crown from winter wet.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the soil surface and press it in, as light aids germination. The aromatic grey-green foliage forms a tidy mound topped by dense, rounded clusters of small creamy-white flowers. Give it sun and dry soil, and shear spent heads to keep the planting neat.

planting.note: Start indoors well before last frost for first-year bloom, then transplant out.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and transplant seedlings out after frost; this compact seed strain flowers in its first year. It forms a dense, low mound of ferny foliage well suited to the front of the border. Deep red flat heads fade gracefully over summer, and deadheading keeps new blooms coming.

planting.note: Start indoors well before last frost for first-year bloom, then transplant out.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of the tray and grow on indoors before setting plants out after frost. This dwarf, uniform strain makes a neat mound of grey-green ferny leaves. Bright yellow flower plates hold well through summer heat; shear lightly to encourage repeat bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors well before last frost for first-year bloom, then transplant out.

directions: Sow on the surface indoors and transplant after frost for bloom the first season. Warm terracotta-orange flowers shift through shades of coral and gold as they age atop a tidy dwarf mound. Give it full sun and lean soil, deadheading spent plates to keep the color coming.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Set this vigorous selection out from division in full sun and average, well-drained soil. Strong, upright stems carry large flat heads of soft lemon-yellow above grey-green foliage. It stands up well without staking; cut spent stems to the base to tidy the clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant divisions in a sunny, well-drained bed where the tall stems have room to stand. Broad flower plates open in pale silvery lavender-pink and soften further as they mature. Its sturdy habit resists flopping; deadhead to prolong the summer show and cut back in fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Set divisions in full sun and lean, free-draining soil for the strongest stems and color. Large flat heads open a rich wine-red and fade through rose to dusky pink for a two-toned effect. Remove faded heads to keep it blooming and shear the whole plant back after the main flush.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant this dwarf selection from division in full sun and average, well-drained soil. It forms a compact, tidy mound topped by rich red flat flower heads that fade softly with age. Its low, dense habit suits the border front and containers; deadhead to keep new blooms rising.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Set divisions in full sun and lean, well-drained soil for the best flower color. Flat heads open in warm golden-orange and mellow toward soft yellow as the blooms age. Deadhead through summer to extend the display and divide the clump every few years to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant divisions in a sunny, free-draining bed and avoid overly rich soil that causes flopping. Broad flat heads open deep wine-red and soften to dusky rose-pink over the flowering season. Cut back after the first flush and remove faded heads to encourage a return bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and press it in, keeping the bed moist until seedlings appear. Woolly grey-green foliage sets off dense clusters of papery white flower heads with yellow centers in late summer. Cut stems just as the heads open to dry them for long-lasting everlasting bouquets.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in reliably moist soil.

directions: Set divisions in full sun to light shade in soil that stays evenly moist through summer. The silvery, felted leaves form a neat clump topped by clusters of pearly-white papery flowers with small yellow eyes. Cut the heads for drying as they open, and shear the plant back after bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant divisions of this free-flowering selection in sun or part shade and evenly moist soil. Silvery foliage carries an abundance of bright white papery flower clusters over a long summer season. Harvest the heads as they open for drying, and divide clumps every few years to keep them dense.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in cool, moist but well-drained soil.

directions: Set divisions in a cool spot with light shade and gritty, humus-rich soil that never bakes dry. This low Himalayan species forms silvery-green mats topped by white papery flowers with golden centers. It prefers cool summers; keep roots moist and lift and divide congested clumps in spring.

planting.note: Set out established divisions in spring once the ground has warmed and moisture is reliable.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil surface in a shaded or dappled spot with steady moisture. The narrow, hair-fine green blades arch into a soft fountain that reads almost white from a distance. Comb out old foliage in late winter and divide clumps every few years to keep them dense.

planting.note: Transplant nursery clumps in spring so roots settle before the heat of summer.

directions: Site this EverColor selection where it gets morning light and afternoon shade, keeping the roots evenly moist. Each blade carries a bright lime-yellow margin around a green center, holding color through most of the year. Shear tired leaves in early spring and it flushes fresh growth quickly.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil for the best foliage color.

directions: Give this golden EverColor sedge dappled shade and consistent water to prevent leaf-tip browning. The blades show a wide gold band edged in green, forming a low arching mound that brightens dim corners. Remove ragged foliage at winter's end and topdress with compost.

planting.note: Set out in spring in part shade with reliably moist, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant this glossy variegated sedge with the crown at grade in moisture-retentive soil under high shade. Bright chartreuse-centered blades edged in green arch outward in an even, tidy mound. Cut back battered leaves in late winter and keep it watered through dry stretches.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost in a sunny, sharply drained spot for the strongest orange tones.

directions: Grow this New Zealand selection in full sun to intensify the fiery orange leaf tips over an olive-green base. Set plants in gritty, free-draining soil and avoid winter wet, which is its main weakness. Rake out dead blades by hand rather than shearing hard, since it resents heavy cutting.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in full sun to part shade with good drainage.

directions: This selection forms a cascading mound of fine silvery-green hairlike foliage that spills nicely over container edges. Plant in well-drained soil and keep lightly moist while establishing, then treat as drought-tolerant. Comb out browned strands in spring instead of cutting the whole clump back.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into moist, fertile soil in part shade.

directions: Plant this fine-textured sedge where its narrow white-edged green blades can arch into a neat mound. It tolerates deeper shade than many variegated cultivars while keeping crisp coloring. Keep the soil evenly moist and tidy the clump by combing out old leaves late in winter.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in a shaded bed with rich, moisture-holding soil.

directions: This low, slowly spreading sedge carries short broad leaves striped cream and green, ideal for shady groundcover. Plant in humus-rich soil and keep the roots cool and moist. It creeps gently by rhizomes to knit together over a couple of seasons; trim ragged foliage in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring near water or in moist ground where it has room to reach full size.

directions: This bold western sedge builds a large fountain of blue-gray, sawtooth-edged leaves and does best beside a pond or in a rain garden. Give it full sun and steady moisture; it also endures periodic dryness once deep-rooted. Cut spent flower stalks and rake the clump clean in late winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in wet ground or along pond margins.

directions: A tough Pacific Northwest wetland sedge that spreads by rhizomes to form dense evergreen colonies for bank stabilization. Plant in saturated to seasonally flooded soil in sun or part shade. It needs little care once settled; mow or cut old growth in late winter if you want a cleaner look.

planting.note: Plant in fall in mild-winter regions so it roots in with the wet season.

directions: A drought-tolerant western sedge that makes a fine-textured green tuft or unmowed meadow-style lawn. Plant in average soil in sun or light shade and water occasionally through summer. It stays low and tidy; a once-a-year comb-out or light mow keeps it fresh.

planting.note: Plant plugs in spring, spacing closely for a knit-together lawn substitute.

directions: This spreading rhizomatous sedge is widely used as a low-water lawn alternative that can be left shaggy or mowed short. Plant plugs on tight centers in sun to part shade and water to establish. Once rooted it tolerates drought and foot traffic; mow a few times a year for a turf look.

planting.note: Plant plugs in spring in sandy, well-drained soil for a meadow lawn.

directions: A coastal California sedge that creeps to form a dense, dark-green low turf tolerant of sand and salt spray. Plant plugs close together in full sun and irrigate until knit in, then reduce water sharply. Mow occasionally for a manicured look or leave it to flow as a soft meadow.

planting.note: Plant at the water's edge in spring; contain the roots to check its spread.

directions: A vigorous European wetland sedge with broad blue-green leaves and dark brown flower spikes, good for large pond margins. Plant in shallow water or boggy soil in sun to part shade. It runs aggressively by rhizomes, so use a planting basket or edging where space is limited.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet ground or shallow water at a pond edge.

directions: This wetland sedge forms spreading stands of arching gray-green blades topped by slender brownish flower spikes. Plant in permanently moist to shallow-flooded soil in sun or light shade. It colonizes by rhizomes, making it useful for naturalizing damp ground where you want cover.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shallow water or saturated soil at a pond margin.

directions: Prized for its drooping, bottlebrush-like green flower spikes that dangle on thin stalks above the foliage. Plant at the water's edge or in a bog garden in sun to part shade. Keep the roots wet at all times and cut back old growth in late winter to show off the new spikes.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist woodland soil under trees.

directions: A graceful shade sedge with soft green blades and long nodding flower spikes that hang like slender green tails. Plant in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil beneath a canopy. It self-sows modestly in congenial spots; comb out old foliage in spring to keep clumps neat.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in average, well-drained soil.

directions: This low mop-headed cultivar forms tidy tufts of narrow dark-green leaves that flop outward like a shaggy haircut. Plant in ordinary garden soil in sun or part shade as an edging or small-scale groundcover. It needs almost no upkeep beyond a spring tidy of any winter-worn blades.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in cool, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: A dainty dwarf sedge with narrow white-striped leaves that stay under a few inches tall, perfect for the front of a shade bed or trough. Plant in gritty, humus-rich soil that never bakes dry. Keep it cool and lightly moist, and remove tatty leaves as the new spring growth appears.

planting.note: Plant after frost in rich, moist soil in a sheltered spot.

directions: A bold coastal sedge from New Zealand with wide, arching blue-green leaves and stout stems bearing chunky flower spikes. Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in sun or part shade, sheltered from cold drying winds. It makes a strong evergreen accent; groom out old leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant after frost in moist but well-drained soil for the richest color.

directions: This New Zealand sedge shifts from olive-green to warm bronze and gold as the seasons turn, holding good color in sun. Plant in fertile, evenly moist soil that drains freely. It resents heavy pruning, so comb out spent blades by hand rather than shearing the clump.

planting.note: Plant after frost in sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: A compact New Zealand sedge forming a low tuft of curled, pinkish-bronze blades that curve toward the ground. Plant in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun and avoid winter wet. It works well in containers and gravel gardens; tidy by pulling out loose dead strands in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, gritty soil in a trough or rock garden.

directions: A tiny creeping New Zealand sedge with broad, coppery-bronze leaves that hug the ground in a flat mat. Plant in moisture-retentive but gritty soil in sun to light shade, ideal for troughs and between paving. It spreads slowly by short runners; simply pick off dead leaves as needed.

planting.note: Plant after frost in wet ground where it can form a tall clump over time.

directions: A striking New Zealand wetland sedge that in time builds a woody trunk-like base topped with a weeping mane of fine blades. Plant in constantly moist to boggy soil in full sun to part shade. Give it years and space to develop its distinctive pedestal habit; little pruning is needed.

planting.note: Plant well after frost in warm regions in rich, moist soil.

directions: This tropical Asian sedge is grown for its arching stems hung with clusters of glossy red to crimson seed-like fruits. Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in sun to part shade where winters stay mild. Enjoy the berry-like display in autumn; cut spent stems back once they fade.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet meadow or pond-edge soil in full sun.

directions: Named for its spiky, bristly seed heads that resemble little burrs, this native wetland sedge suits rain gardens and pond margins. Plant in saturated, fertile soil in full sun to light shade. It reseeds readily in wet ground, filling in bare spots and feeding wetland wildlife.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shallow water or marshy ground.

directions: A robust native marsh sedge that spreads to form thick colonies with inflated, beaked seed capsules along the spikes. Plant in permanently wet soil or shallow standing water in sun to part shade. It excels at stabilizing shorelines and filtering runoff; expect vigorous rhizomatous spread.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet meadow soil or along a stream.

directions: A western wetland sedge with blue-green blades that forms dense sod for streambank and wet-meadow restoration. Plant in saturated soil in full sun; it tolerates seasonal flooding and grazing. Its strong rhizomes bind soil quickly, so give it room to run.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shallow water or perpetually wet ground.

directions: An extremely cold-hardy circumboreal sedge of marshes and lake shores, forming spreading stands of blue-green foliage. Plant in shallow water or saturated soil in full sun to light shade. It thrives in cold climates and seasonal flooding, spreading steadily by rhizomes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, lime-rich soil at a pond or bog edge.

directions: A tufted wetland sedge whose plump, yellow-green seed heads bristle outward like tiny stars in summer. Plant in consistently moist, preferably alkaline soil in full sun to part shade. It stays clump-forming rather than running, so it fits smaller bog and pondside plantings.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average to damp soil; it adapts to many sites.

directions: This adaptable European sedge is easily told by the soft hairs on its leaves and sheaths, and it copes with dry or damp ground alike. Plant in ordinary soil in sun or part shade. It spreads by rhizomes to cover rough ground, so give it space or contain it in a bed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sandy, free-draining soil for dune-style stabilization.

directions: A coastal dune sedge famous for its long straight rhizomes that send up shoots in neat rows as they travel through sand. Plant in poor, sharply drained soil in full sun where its running habit is an asset. It knits loose sand together, so use it for stabilization, not tidy borders.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist grassland or meadow soil in sun.

directions: A tufted meadow sedge, also called hare's-foot sedge, with clusters of egg-shaped brown flower heads bunched at the stem tips. Plant in moist, average soil in full sun to light shade. It is a tidy clump-former good for wildflower meadows; leave the seed heads for texture and wildlife.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, alkaline soil in sun or part shade.

directions: A clump-forming native sedge with blue-green leaves and chubby, granular-looking green seed spikes, suited to wet meadows and rain gardens. Plant in moist, preferably limy soil in sun to part shade. It tolerates clay and seasonal wetness; it is well behaved and needs little maintenance.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich woodland soil under a shade canopy.

directions: A refined native woodland sedge with soft blue-green blades that spreads gently to form an even groundcover in shade. Plant in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil beneath trees. Its early spring flush and slow rhizomatous spread make it a fine understory carpet with little care needed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dry to average woodland soil in shade.

directions: Grown for its unusually wide, powder-blue leaves that stand out among finer woodland sedges. Plant in well-drained, humus-rich soil in shade to part shade; it tolerates dry shade once established. Its slow-spreading clumps make a handsome low accent under trees with minimal upkeep.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist meadow or wetland-edge soil in sun.

directions: A tidy native wet-meadow sedge topped with rounded clusters of small brown seed heads that catch the light. Plant in moist to wet soil in full sun to light shade. It clumps rather than runs and reseeds gently, making it a dependable choice for rain gardens and pond surrounds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average to moist soil in sun or part shade.

directions: A short, fine-textured native sedge that adapts to lawns, meadows, and shady groundcover plantings. Plant in ordinary soil in sun to part shade; it withstands mowing and light foot traffic. It self-sows to fill in over time, making it useful as a low-care green carpet.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist woodland soil in shade to part shade.

directions: A delicate native woodland sedge forming airy fountains of thread-fine green blades with small star-shaped seed clusters. Plant in humus-rich, evenly moist soil in shade to dappled light. It makes a soft, fine-textured groundcover under trees and self-sows lightly to naturalize.

common_name: Kleine Silberspinne Maiden Grass (Little Silver Spider)

planting.note: Set out divisions after the last frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant the crown at the depth it grew in the pot, spacing clumps far enough apart to show off the fine, silvery foliage. Water regularly the first season, then only during dry spells once established. Reddish-silver plumes rise above the leaves in late summer and hold into winter, so leave the clump standing and cut it to the ground in early spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost so roots establish through the warm season.

directions: Dig a hole as deep as the root ball and set the plant level with the surrounding grade in fertile, well-drained soil. Keep it evenly moist while it settles, then reduce watering as the clump fills out. Fine blue-green blades carry a narrow silver midrib and are topped by coppery plumes that fade to silver in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring to give this tall selection a full season to reach flowering size.

directions: Give this large grass room and full sun, planting the crown flush with the soil surface. It tolerates a range of soils but performs best with steady moisture until well rooted. Tall arching foliage is topped in autumn by burgundy-tinged plumes that mature to silvery tan and persist through winter.

planting.note: Set out after frost; this compact variegated form suits smaller beds and containers.

directions: Plant the division at its original depth in well-drained soil and water in thoroughly. This is a dwarf, white-banded version of Variegated Maiden Grass that holds a tidier habit and rarely flops. Coppery plumes appear in fall above the crisp cream-and-green blades; cut the clump back before new growth begins in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; its small size fits borders, edging, and pots.

directions: Dig a hole matching the root ball and set the crown level with the soil, firming gently. One of the most compact maiden grasses, it forms a neat mound of narrow green blades. Small silvery plumes emerge in late summer above the foliage; shear the clump close to the ground in early spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring in full sun for the best plume display.

directions: Plant the crown flush with the grade and water steadily until established. This selection is prized for large, arching pink-tinted flower heads that seem to cascade from the tall foliage. The plumes open in late summer and fade to silver; leave them for winter interest and cut back in spring.

planting.note: Give this very tall grass a spring planting and plenty of space as a screen or specimen.

directions: Site this towering grass in full sun with room to spread and plant the crown at soil level. Keep it watered the first year, after which it is fairly drought tolerant. Broad reddish plumes rise well above the strong upright foliage in late summer, turning silvery by fall and standing through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this compact white-variegated form works well in mixed borders.

directions: Set the division at its former depth in well-drained soil and water in well. A more restrained, white-striped selection that keeps its variegation crisp through the season. Reddish plumes fade to silver in autumn; cut the clump back to a few inches in early spring before growth resumes.
